mci.subnational: district-level: Jaffna district only (1 of 25 districts; Northern Province)
mci.representative: regional/community — single-district community sample (Jaffna, Tamil-speaking Northern Province); NOT nationally representative
mci.n: ~1000 (estimated from 95% CI; exact n only in unpublished thesis)

scd.criteria: self-reported cognitive complaints among non-demented (structured population survey question) — complaints-based, inflates SCD
scd.citation: Perquin et al. Prevalence of Dementia and Cognitive Complaints in the Context of High Cognitive Reserve: A Population-Based Study (MemoVie, Luxembourg). PLOS ONE 2015;10(9):e0138818. n=1377 residents aged >64; cognitive complaints 26% among those without dementia (dementia 3.8%). NOTE: MemoVie protocol (BMC Public Health 2012, 10.1186/1471-2458-12-519) gave only a design-EXPECTED MCI ~20%; no published actual MCI point prevalence located, so MCI set found=false.
scd.representative: national (Luxembourg residents >64, n=1377)

mci.criteria: Validated telephone-administered cognitive screening questionnaire (specific tool not named in abstract; MCI examined as dementia precursor). NOT MoCA/MMSE/Petersen-verified in searchable text.
mci.citation: Chahidi A, Souirti Z, Belarbi N, Boujraf S. Unveiling the Dementia Burden: A National Prevalence Study in Morocco. Alzheimer's & Dementia 2025 (conference proceedings; PMC12726269). Large-scale cross-sectional national study, nationally representative sample of adults 50+ (urban+rural), telephone-administered validated cognitive screening; estimates dementia, Alzheimer's, and MCI, and aims to identify regional/demographic variations. VERIFIED source is real, on-point, and open-access, BUT the numeric MCI point-prevalence is NOT reported in the searchable abstract (background/methods/objectives only) - need full text to extract the value; WebFetch blocked so value could not be captured without fabrication. No better Morocco-specific MCI figure found: Sedighi 2026 MENA meta-analysis explicitly discarded Morocco (hospital-based only); Africa MCI meta-analysis (Nat Transl Psychiatry 2025, s41398-025-03620-z) gives no Morocco-specific estimate.
mci.representative: national (nationally representative sample, urban+rural, adults 50+; full-text likely holds regional breakdowns but values not extractable via search)

scd.criteria: structured-questionnaire (McNair-Kahn cognitive-complaints scale; self-reported cognitive complaints). Value ~51.6% is inflated, consistent with SCD self-report over-report tendency (40-60%); primary-care/clinic sample, single city.
scd.citation: El Harsi EM, Benksim A, Cherkaoui M. Cognitive complaints in older adults in primary care and associated factors. Dement Neuropsychol. 2023 (PMC10229085). Cross-sectional study in primary health care centers, Marrakech, Mar-Jun 2022; n=281 adults aged 50+; 51.6% reported cognitive complaints on the McNair-Kahn scale. Clinic-based, single-city sample - not nationally representative; inflated per SCD self-report trap. Seed VERIFIED and numeric value extracted.
scd.representative: clinic (primary health care centers, Marrakech only; not nationally representative)

mci.criteria: HDS-R with <=20 cutoff; 'reduced/impaired cognitive function' = broad cognitive impairment (dementia-range screen), NOT DSM-5 MCI — overestimates and conflates dementia; tool recorded
mci.citation: Cognitive impairment and its risk factors among Myanmar elderly using the Revised Hasegawa's Dementia Scale: a cross-sectional study in Nay Pyi Taw, Myanmar. PLOS ONE. 2020;15(7):e0236656. Data collected Dec 2018-Jan 2019 at rural health centres. n=757; 29.9% impaired (men 23.6%, women 32.9%).
mci.subnational: Nay Pyi Taw Union Territory, rural health centres only; sex-stratified
mci.representative: community/regional (rural Nay Pyi Taw); not national

mci.criteria: local screening scale (specific tool not stated in overview); 'mild CI' — proxy for MCI, secondary/second-hand citation, NOT verified DSM-5/Petersen; confidence low
mci.citation: Vascular cognitive impairment risk among Mongolian adults: an overview. Neuroscience Research Notes. 2021;4(3 Suppl):99. Cites local Mongolian studies: Otgonbayar 2017 (Ulaanbaatar elderly) 27.0% mild CI (+5.0% moderate); Oyunkhand 2011 (>60) 39.7% any CI of which ~59.5% mild (~23.6%). Local/dissertation sources, no international primary publication — need full text.
mci.subnational: Ulaanbaatar (Otgonbayar 2017); national elderly (Oyunkhand 2011)
mci.representative: community; only locally-published/dissertation studies, no international primary source

scd.criteria: self-report-single-item (self-reported memory problems) — classic SCD single-question inflation (>60%). Objective cognitive impairment (AMT<7) was only 11.2% in same sample, underscoring inflation. Prefer regional/structured data over this.
scd.citation: Falls and other geriatric syndromes in Blantyre, Malawi: a community survey of older adults. Malawi Med J 2014;26(4):105-108. 66.3% self-reported memory problems; 11.2% AMT<7. Sample size not captured in abstract — need full text.
scd.representative: community (Blantyre, urban — not national)

mci.criteria: HCAP harmonized algorithmic classification (Mex-Cog/HCAP battery incl. informant AD8) — NOT MoCA-only, so avoids the ~20%+ MoCA overestimation; dementia co-estimated at 6.7%
mci.citation: Prevalence of dementia and mild cognitive impairment in the Mexican population: evidence from Mex-Cog, a Harmonized Cognitive Assessment Protocol Study. J Gerontol B Psychol Sci Soc Sci 2026;81(3):gbaf268. MCI 10% (95% CI 8.7–11.7); dementia 6.7% (95% CI 5.4–8.0). Analytic n=1,629 aged 60+, 2016 Mex-Cog wave (MHAS ancillary, HCAP design), nationally representative with sampling weights. Risk factors for MCI: older age, female sex, lower education, rural residence, lack of health insurance. DOI 10.1093/geronb/gbaf268. Improves on prior audit seed MHAS 2011 (PMC3557523), which reported CIND 28.7% — a broader construct on older 2001/2003 data; corroborated by medRxiv 2020 MHAS MCI preprint (also ~10%, 95% CI 8.7–11.7).
mci.access: paywalled(Oxford Academic); green OA mirror at scholarworks.utrgv.edu/som_pub/2015
mci.representative: national (MHAS/Mex-Cog nationally representative sub-sample of adults 60+, sampling-weighted)

scd.criteria: structured-questionnaire — 'with subjective memory complaint' (WSMC) categorization via clinical criteria + AD8 informant interview + expert consensus among cognitively healthy participants (MoCA-T screen, GDS-15). NOT SCD-I framework and NOT Mexico-representative
scd.citation: Caldichoury N, et al. Subjective memory complaints in Latin American older adults: prevalence and risk factors. J Alzheimers Dis 2025. SMC ~18.3% (602 of 3,285) pooled across 10 Latin American/Caribbean countries INCLUDING Mexico; COVID-era (Jun–Oct 2020) multicenter convenience sample. NO Mexico-specific breakdown reported in abstract. No nationally representative Mexico SCD/SMC prevalence found (SAGE-Mexico and Mexican-American US studies treat SMC as a covariate, not a national prevalence). Recorded as regional triangulation only. DOI 10.1177/13872877251400666.
scd.representative: regional (10-country LatAm/Caribbean pooled multicenter convenience sample, includes but not specific to Mexico; not national)

mci.criteria: Neuropsychological test battery, community MCI (Petersen-type). NOT MoCA-only. Malaysian MoCA-only studies grossly overestimate MCI (MoCA-BM primary-care 55.6%; MELoR MoCA-Blind cognitive impairment 66.3%); M-MMSE 32.8%; rural objective CI ~11%. Range across Malaysian cohorts 16-30%.
mci.citation: Rivan NFM, Shahar S, Rajab NF, et al. Cognitive frailty among Malaysian older adults: baseline findings from the LRGS-TUA (Towards Useful Ageing) cohort study. Clinical Interventions in Aging. 2019;14. Community-dwelling adults 60+; MCI prevalence ~16% via full neuropsychological assessment. Consistent with global community pooled ~17.9%. 2016 Dusunen Adam MCI-screening seed (dusunenadamdergisi.org/article/111) verified as a lower-priority screening study, superseded here.
mci.subnational: state-level; multi-state community sample (Selangor, Negeri Sembilan, Perak, Kelantan) — 4 of 16 states
mci.representative: community — multi-state community-dwelling older adults, not nationally representative

mci.criteria: UDS-3 multi-domain neuropsychological composite with consensus classification (Petersen-style, NOT MoCA-only — more conservative); amnestic MCI subtype 2.72%
mci.citation: Prevalence and Patterns of Cognitive Impairment in a Sample of Community Dwelling Older People in Nigeria (2024), Anambra State; n=441 community-dwelling adults aged 60-87; UDS-3 5-domain composite: normal 49.7%, borderline 34%, MCI 12.9% (amnestic 2.72%), dementia 3.4%. Triangulation: rural SW Nigeria IDEA cognitive screen MCI 18.1% (n=613, age 65+, PMC4951621, Olakehinde/Adebiyi 2016); Africa MCI systematic review/meta-analysis pooled ~34.5% post-2019 (Nature Transl Psychiatry 2025, s41398-025-03620-z) but MoCA-inflated. Also ScienceDirect S2534773X24000429.
mci.subnational: state-level community samples only (Anambra State; rural SW Nigeria; Ibadan/Yoruba areas) — no systematic national admin-level breakdown
mci.representative: community (single-state community sample, Anambra; not nationally representative)

mci.criteria: Petersen (subjective complaint + objective neuropsychological impairment, preserved function, no dementia); NOT MoCA-based
mci.citation: de Bruijn RFAG, Akoudad S, Cremers LGM, et al. Determinants, MRI Correlates, and Prognosis of Mild Cognitive Impairment: The Rotterdam Study. J Alzheimers Dis. 2014;42(3):855-864. MCI prevalence 10.0% (417/4198 non-demented; 163 amnestic [~3.9%], 254 non-amnestic [~6.1%]). Verified seed.
mci.representative: regional (Ommoord district, Rotterdam); large population-based cohort, community-dwelling

scd.criteria: self-report-single-item (memory complaints question, cognitively intact at baseline)
scd.citation: Dik MG, Jonker C, Comijs HC, et al. Memory complaints and APOE-e4 accelerate cognitive decline in cognitively normal elderly. Neurology. 2001;57(12):2217-2222 (LASA, Longitudinal Aging Study Amsterdam, nationally representative Dutch cohort). Baseline memory complaints in 25.5% of cognitively intact elderly (n=1168, 62-85). Improves on Rotterdam SMC seed (PMID 19595922), which reports SMC as an AD risk factor (HR ~1.5-2.3) not a clean point prevalence. Triangulates with COSMIC pooled SCD-I estimate 25.6% (Rohr 2020, doi 10.1186/s13195-020-00734-y). Note: Netherlands community 34.3% (65-85, Jonker via Frontiers review doi 10.3389/fneur.2020.00247) is single-item/higher and older - need full text.
scd.representative: national (nationally representative sample of Dutch older adults, LASA)

mci.criteria: DSM-5 mild neurocognitive disorder; clinical consensus diagnosis by two of nine specialists (geriatrics/neurology/old-age psychiatry) using full cognitive assessment, ADL, neuropsychiatric symptoms, disease course + caregiver interview. NOT MoCA-only or MMSE-only, so no tool-driven over/under-estimation. Note: 35.3% is high because DSM-5 mild NCD captures a broad MCI category in the 70+ age band.
mci.citation: Gjora L, Strand BH, Bergh S, et al. Current and Future Prevalence Estimates of Mild Cognitive Impairment, Dementia, and Its Subtypes in a Population-Based Sample of People 70 Years and Older in Norway: The HUNT Study. J Alzheimers Dis. 2021;79(3):1213-1226. HUNT4 70+, n=9663 with sufficient data. Standardized prevalence 35.3% (95% CI 34.3-36.4).
mci.representative: national (population-based HUNT4 70+ Trondelag/Nord-Trondelag sample standardized to Norwegian population; all invited 70+ n=19403, 51.2% participated)

scd.criteria: structured-questionnaire / clinic diagnostic workup, but figure is CLINIC CASE-MIX not population prevalence; the 13% combines 'no cognitive impairment (NCI)' AND 'subjective cognitive impairment (SCI)' at baseline, so it is not a pure SCD figure. NOT a single self-report item and NOT SCD-I framework. Clinic-referral denominator inflates/deflates unpredictably vs general population.
scd.citation: Medbol RS/Gjora L et al. (NorCog). Cohort profile: the Norwegian Registry of Persons Assessed for Cognitive Symptoms (NorCog) - a national research and quality registry with a biomaterial collection. BMJ Open. 2022;12(9):e058810. Of 18120 patients assessed 2009-2021: 46% dementia, 35% MCI, 13% no/subjective cognitive impairment (NCI+SCI combined), 7% other. Clinic-referral sample, NOT population prevalence - use as clinic proxy only.
scd.representative: clinic (national memory-clinic/specialist-healthcare referral registry; not a general-population sample)

mci.criteria: MoCA <26 using publisher cut-off; NO linguistic/education adaptation despite 81.7% with no formal education; MCI NOT differentiated from dementia — MoCA-only screen, severe overestimate
mci.citation: Pradhan N, Adhikari BR, Lama S, Shrestha S, Sigdel R. Prevalence of Mild-Cognitive-Impairment and Depression among elderly clients in selected wards of Baraha Municipality: a cross-sectional study. J BP Koirala Inst Health Sci. 2020;3(1):57-65. NOTE: 93.0% is a severe overestimate (MoCA<26 publisher cut-off, no education/linguistic adaptation, MCI not separated from dementia); it is the only Nepal-specific MCI figure. Triangulation: PLOS One 2025 'Risk of dementia in Nepal' (n=933) found 53.7% suspected cognitive impairment/dementia risk and dementia 8.8-10.7% (Brief-CSID/IQCODE) but no validated MCI-specific rate. No Petersen/DSM-5-criteria MCI prevalence exists for Nepal.
mci.subnational: municipality (Baraha Municipality, selected wards; 1 unit)
mci.representative: community (single municipality, n=115; not nationally representative)

mci.ci: Cognitive impairment (3MS≤77): non-Māori 6.4% (n=25); Māori 15.1% (n=39). No pooled CI reported
mci.criteria: 3MS ≤77 global cognitive screen (MMSE-family) = broad cognitive impairment (dementia+MCI), NOT Petersen MCI; MMSE-family underestimates mild MCI; ethnic-calibrated cutoffs used (~5pts higher than standard). No national Petersen/DSM-5 MCI survey exists for NZ
mci.source_type: national-cohort (LiLACS NZ advanced-age cohort; regionally recruited)
mci.citation: Cheung G et al. Diagnostic accuracy of a global cognitive screen (3MS) for Māori and non-Māori octogenarians, LiLACS NZ. Cognitive impairment (3MS≤77): Māori 15.1% (n=39), non-Māori 6.4% (n=25). NOTE: this is 3MS-screen cognitive impairment in octogenarians, not general-65+ Petersen MCI. LiDiA seed (Martinez-Ruiz 2024, PMC11041074) VERIFIED but feasibility-only (n=75, 25.2% completion, high attrition) — reports response rates, no usable MCI/dementia prevalence value. No national MCI prevalence survey exists for NZ
mci.subnational: 2 DHB regions (Bay of Plenty + Lakes/Rotorua) — LiLACS recruitment area; ethnic-specific figures available (Māori vs non-Māori)
mci.representative: regional cohort (Bay of Plenty/Lakes DHB), advanced-age octogenarians; NOT representative of general NZ 65+; over-represents oldest-old
mci.n: ~649 with cognition data (258 Māori, 391 non-Māori); LiLACS baseline ~937 (421 Māori, 516 non-Māori)

scd.criteria: Self-reported subjective cognitive impairment/complaints — INFLATED (self-report, typical 40–60% range; not structured SCD-I framework). Sample restricted to cognitively-healthy older WOMEN, highly educated (mean 16.1 yr). PARI-HD cohort.
scd.citation: Villarreal AE et al. Predictors of cognitive change in cognitively healthy older women in Panama: the PARI-HD study. Front Glob Womens Health. 2024;5:1353657. (43.4% reported subjective cognitive impairment).
scd.representative: community (Panama City), older women only — not national, sex-restricted

mci.age: 40-85 (community adults; MCI/dementia predominant in older adults)
mci.criteria: RUDAS + INECO Frontal Screening (IFS) two-stage screening; reported as 'possible MCI', NOT gold-standard Petersen/DSM-5 neuropsych consensus. Screening-tool approach OVERESTIMATES MCI; includes adults 40+ not just 65+.
mci.citation: Zegarra-Valdivia J, et al. Prevalence and risk factors of mild cognitive impairment and dementia in northern Peru. Front Public Health. 2025;13:1567073. Two-stage community study, Chiclayo (Lambayeque), n=385 aged 40-85: 31.4% normal, 40.5% possible MCI, 24.9% possible dementia; risk factors age, low education, hypertension, hearing loss. CAVEAT: RUDAS+IFS screening (not Petersen) inflates MCI. Triangulation: Custodio et al, amnestic MCI at 4 Lima senior-citizen clubs = 17.9% by Petersen criteria, n=352 (PMC6753901); ~8 Peru studies over 20 yrs range 6.85-23.96%.
mci.subnational: single-city study (Chiclayo, Lambayeque region); no multi-admin-unit sub-national breakdown
mci.representative: community (single urban cohort, Chiclayo; not nationally representative)

scd.criteria: subjective memory complaints (SMC), self-report; NOT SCD-I structured framework. Single-item/self-report SMC inflates estimates.
scd.citation: Caldichoury N, Morales-Asencio B, Coronado JC, et al. Subjective memory complaints in Latin American older adults: Prevalence and risk factors. J Alzheimers Dis. 2025. Multi-country LatAm study; Peru had the HIGHEST national SMC prevalence at 26.30% vs regional mean 18.33%. Peru-specific sample size not extractable from abstract - need full text. (Smaller Peru correlate study: Arequipa n=43, SCD n=17, PMC13105449.)
scd.representative: regional (Peru subgroup within multi-country LatAm sample; national point estimate, non-probability)

mci.criteria: Petersen clinical MCI (community consensus, neuropsychological + CDR-based diagnosis; NOT MoCA-only screen, so no MoCA overestimation)
mci.citation: Dominguez J, de Guzman MF, Reandelar M, Phung TKT, Abe K. Prevalence of Dementia and Associated Risk Factors: A Population-Based Study in the Philippines (Marikina Memory and Aging Project, MMAP). J Alzheimers Dis. 2018;63(3):1065-1073. MCI prevalence 23.2% among adults aged 60+ (dementia 10.6%). Single-city community sample, Marikina City, Metro Manila. Figure corroborated by open-access FINOMAIN protocol (PMC8453078) which cites the same 23.2% MCI.
mci.access: paywalled(SAGE/IOS Press) — abstract carries dementia figure; 23.2% MCI corroborated in open-access FINOMAIN protocol PMC8453078
mci.subnational: city-level: 1 unit (Marikina City, Metro Manila) — not national
mci.representative: community (single urban city cohort; not nationally representative)

mci.criteria: MoCA-Urdu, standard cutoff <26 for MCI (MoCA-only — OVERESTIMATES MCI; 30% is inflated vs Asian pooled ~25% / global 65+ ~13-17%)
mci.citation: Prevalence of Cognitive Impairment and Risk Factors among Elderly in Rawalpindi, Pakistan: A Cross-Sectional Study. J Bahria Uni Med Dental Coll. 2024;14(2):123-7. Overall cognitive impairment 35.4%; MCI 134 (30%), dementia 24 (5.4%); females higher (61%). Single-city community sample. DOI not verified via search — need full text.
mci.subnational: city — Rawalpindi (1 city, Punjab province); no nationwide sub-national breakdown
mci.representative: community; single-city cross-sectional sample, NOT nationally representative

mci.criteria: MMSE age/education-corrected, band 24.0-26.99 = 'suspected MCI' (MMSE-based, not Petersen/DSM-5 neuropsych; MMSE typically underestimates MCI, but here defined by MMSE cut-band)
mci.citation: PolSenior2 study: 'The cross-sectional relationship of physical frailty and cognitive impairment in older Poles: analysis of the PolSenior2 data', European Geriatric Medicine (2025). Nationwide community-dwelling Poles aged 60+, n=5378 (58.0% women, mean age 75.0), MMSE age/education-corrected, population-weighted with age-sex post-stratification to Poland: 23.0% suspected MCI (51.3% normal; 16.3% mild, 6.5% moderate, 2.9% severe dementia). Triangulation: earlier PolSenior (2014, PMID 24937034) reported 12.1% adjusted-MMSE cognitive impairment suspected of dementia (more dementia-oriented, not MCI).
mci.representative: national (community-dwelling adults 60+, population-weighted to Poland; PMC12378653)

mci.criteria: 10/66 aMCI (Mayo Clinic amnestic MCI algorithm) — strict amnestic-only, UNDERESTIMATES all-type MCI. Pooled crude aMCI 0.8–4.3% (standardized 0.6–4.6%); exact Puerto Rico value in full-text Table 1 — need full text. Caribbean Hispanics have relatively HIGH AD/MCI incidence (PREHCO cohort context).
mci.citation: Sosa AL et al. 10/66 MCI study, PLOS Med. 2012;9(2):e1001170 (Puerto Rico catchment). Related cohort: PREHCO (Puerto Rican Elderly: Health Conditions), 60+ island-wide. Need full text for exact PR aMCI %.
mci.representative: national/island (10/66 Puerto Rico; PREHCO 60+ island-wide)

mci.criteria: MoCA (validated Arabic version) — MoCA OVERestimates MCI
mci.citation: Factors associated with mild cognitive impairment among Palestinian community-dwelling older adults (Hebron governorate), Cogent Psychology 2025 (DOI 10.1080/23311908.2025.2478715); 358 adults >=60, Arabic MoCA, MCI 37.3%
mci.representative: regional/community (Hebron governorate, West Bank)

mci.criteria: Petersen clinical criteria; MMSE + MoCA screening then confirmatory clinical diagnosis (adjusted prevalence). Not MoCA-cutoff-only, so not inflated by the MoCA overestimation trap; likely conservative.
mci.citation: Ruano L, Araujo N, Branco M, et al. Prevalence and Causes of Cognitive Impairment and Dementia in a Population-Based Cohort From Northern Portugal (EPIPorto). Am J Alzheimers Dis Other Demen. 2019;34(1):49-56. Adjusted MCI prevalence 4.1% (dementia 1.3%); most common cause vascular 52.8%, then Alzheimer 36.1%.
mci.subnational: regional - Porto / Northern Portugal (1 city, EPIPorto cohort)
mci.representative: community; single-city population-based cohort (Porto), not nationally representative

mci.criteria: HCAP-validated harmonized cognitive assessment battery (SHARE 2022 wave); NOT MoCA-only, so avoids MoCA overestimation trap. Study-wide MCI range 17.2% (Sweden) to 31.1% (Portugal).
mci.citation: Bacigalupo et al. (2025) 'Harmonized prevalence estimates of dementia in Europe vary strongly with childhood education', Scientific Reports 15. SHARE 2022 wave (n=47,773, age 65+, 27 European countries + Israel) reports MCI + dementia prevalence via an HCAP-validated harmonized battery. MCI ranges 17.2-31.1%; Southeastern/Mediterranean countries reach 'almost a third' (Bulgaria/Cyprus/Greece/Portugal named). Romania IS a participating country and its low childhood-education profile (the paper's key upward driver) places it in the high cluster, but Romania's exact country-specific MCI point value is in the supplementary tables and was NOT in the abstract — need full text to confirm. The 30% here is an approximation from the SE-Europe high-prevalence cluster, not an extracted Romania point value. No domestic Romanian population MCI survey exists (only Vintila 2016 gps.4531 diagnostic paper and a 60-person MoCA exercise study PMC11395204).
mci.representative: national (SHARE nationally-representative probability sample of community-dwelling adults 65+); Romania is a SHARE participating country
mci.n: 47,773 (whole 27-country study; Romania subsample not separately reported in abstract)

mci.ci: no 95% CI for overall CI; severe/dementia-range 19.9% (mini-Cog 0-1 pt)
mci.criteria: mini-Cog screen: 'any cognitive impairment' = MCI + dementia CONFLATED (severe 19.9%); OVERESTIMATES MCI, NOT Petersen/DSM-5 MCI. Non-severe (MCI-range) portion ~40.9% derived. Cleaner regional triangulation: Ural Very Old Study MMSE mild-CI (19-23) = 24.8% (95% CI 22.5-27.0) but 85+ only, MMSE underestimates.
mci.citation: Mkhitaryan EA, Vorobieva NM, Tkacheva ON, Kotovskaya YV, Koberskaya NN, Selezneva EV, Ovcharova LN. Prevalence of cognitive impairment in older adults in the Russian Federation (EVKALIPT study). Zh Nevrol Psikhiatr im SS Korsakova 2024;124(4 Vyp 2):5-x. National epidemiological study, 11 federal regions, n=3545, age >=65, mini-Cog. Any CI 60.8%, severe 19.9%. Same dataset also in Neurology Neuropsychiatry Psychosomatics 2022;14(3):44-53 (DOI 10.14412/2074-2711-2022-3-44-53). Full text paywalled; figures from abstract. Triangulation: Bikbov MM et al. Cognitive impairment in the population-based Ural Very Old Study. Front Aging Neurosci 2022;14:912755 (OA), 85+, n=1442, MMSE mild-CI 24.8%.
mci.representative: national (11 of ~85 federal subjects; community + care-home dwellers 65+); per-region prevalence not broken out

mci.criteria: MoCA (Arabic-validated), standard cutoff — MoCA OVERESTIMATES MCI; value is in the upper range (authors' own note), likely inflated. MMSE-based alternative gives ~17.1% (MMSE underestimates), bracketing true value
mci.citation: Alkhunizan M, Alkhenizan A, Basudan L. Prevalence of Mild Cognitive Impairment and Dementia in Saudi Arabia: A Community-Based Study. Dement Geriatr Cogn Dis Extra. 2018;8(1):98-103. MCI 38.6%, dementia 6.4%; n=171 primary-care outpatients aged 60+, Riyadh; Arabic MoCA. Alternative: MMSE-based ~17.1% MCI (Assessing cognitive status of older adults at PHC centers, Saudi Med J 2020;41(12):1315, n=1299) — MMSE underestimates.
mci.subnational: single-city (Riyadh only, 1 region); not nationally representative
mci.representative: community (clinic-recruited, single-center Family Medicine Clinics, KFSH&RC Riyadh)

scd.criteria: self-report-single-item (memory-problem complaint) — INFLATES SCD; no SCD-I structured framework or validated questionnaire used; figure reported qualitatively as 'almost a third', exact % not stated
scd.citation: Alkhunizan M, Alkhenizan A, Basudan L. Prevalence of Mild Cognitive Impairment and Dementia in Saudi Arabia. Dement Geriatr Cogn Dis Extra. 2018;8(1):98-103. Reports 'almost a third' (~30%) of subjects had memory-problem complaints via single self-report item; n=171, aged 60+, Riyadh. No dedicated SCD prevalence survey identified for Saudi Arabia; use as low-confidence proxy or fall back to regional pooled estimate.
scd.subnational: single-city (Riyadh only, 1 region); not nationally representative
scd.representative: community (clinic-recruited, single-center, Riyadh)

mci.criteria: MoCA <26 (whole-adult community sample, NOT MCI-specific; MoCA overestimates and young mean age further inflates)
mci.citation: Prevalence of and Factors Associated with Cognitive Impairment Among Adults in Central Sudan (East Gezira): a community-based cross-sectional study, Int J Gen Med 2025; MoCA<26 in 336 adults aged >=18, overall cognitive impairment 73.5%
mci.representative: community (single locality, East Gezira; general adults, not older-adult-specific)

mci.ci: 95% CI 15.8-18.0 (age-60 baseline cohort, 482/2900); seed Overton 2019 reports range 5.13-29.9% by age & severity
mci.age: 60+ (16.5% figure from the 60-yr baseline cohort; GÅS enrolls 60 and 81 at exam)
mci.criteria: Expanded Mayo Clinic / Petersen criteria: cognitive complaint + objective impairment on full neuropsychological battery + preserved function + no dementia. NOT MoCA-only, so avoids MoCA overestimation trap; threshold sensitivity (1 vs 1.5 SD) drives the wide range.
mci.citation: Overton M, Pihlsgard M, Elmstahl S. Secular trends in prevalent mild cognitive impairment: Data from the Swedish population-based study Good Aging in Skane (GAS/SNAC-S). Alzheimers Dement (N Y) / A&D TRCI 2022;8:e12260 (overall MCI 16.5%, 95% CI 15.8-18.0). Seed: Overton M, Pihlsgard M, Elmstahl S. Prevalence and Incidence of Mild Cognitive Impairment across Subtypes, Age, and Sex. Dement Geriatr Cogn Disord 2019;47:219-232 (n=3752, 60+, range 5.13-29.9%).
mci.subnational: region: Skane county (SNAC-S / Good Aging in Skane); 1 of 21 Swedish regions, urban+rural sampling. No national breakdown; single-region cohort.
mci.representative: regional (population-based, southern Sweden/Skane; part of the national SNAC program but numeric is Skane-only)
mci.n: 2900 (age-60 baseline, 2022 paper); 3752 aged 60+ in 2019 seed paper

scd.criteria: self-report / structured-questionnaire: subjective complaint of cognitive change WITHOUT objective cognitive impairment (SCI). Broad self-report definition, NOT the SCD-I framework; 39% sits in the inflated 40-60% self-report zone -> treat as an upper-bound.
scd.citation: Caracciolo B, Gatz M, Xu W, Pedersen NL, Fratiglioni L. Differential distribution of subjective and objective cognitive impairment in the population: a nation-wide twin-study. J Alzheimers Dis 2012;29(2):393-403. Nationwide Swedish Twin Registry; SCI 39% (95% CI 38-39), CIND 25% (95% CI 24-25); n=11,926 dementia-free twins >=65.
scd.representative: national (Swedish Twin Registry, nationwide sample of twins; twin cohort may not perfectly mirror singletons)

mci.criteria: Population screening study; 54.7% abnormal screening tests; cognitive screening tool (MMSE-type). Dementia 10.25% in same sample. CONFERENCE ABSTRACT (AAIC 2017) — sampling/methods not fully reported; authors note prevalence HIGHER than other regional projections.
mci.citation: Hernández et al. [P2-525] Prevalence of Mild Cognitive Impairment and Dementia in a Population of Adults Over 60 Years Old in El Salvador. Alzheimer's & Dementia. 2017;13(7S) (conference abstract).
mci.representative: community, adults >60 El Salvador (sampling frame unclear)

mci.criteria: MoCA-based screening (Thai MoCA), Petersen concept — MoCA OVERESTIMATES MCI. Seed DDP 2013 (PMC3552988) verified as national elderly cohort (n=1998, 60-95, Petersen/clinical) but is a design/methodology paper with NO extractable MCI prevalence %. Regional pooled Petersen elderly benchmark ~15.5% for triangulation.
mci.citation: Pinyopornpanish K, et al. (2024). A household survey of the prevalence of subjective cognitive decline and mild cognitive impairment among urban community-dwelling adults aged 30 to 65. Scientific Reports (Nature). MCI = 33.95% of n=539, urban Chiang Mai. MoCA-based (overestimate) + non-elderly age band. More authoritative seed DDP 2013 (Senanarong et al., BMC Neurol, PMC3552988) verified but yields no clean extractable MCI value; national dementia (not MCI) = 3.3% (Jitapunkul, PubMed 11460954).
mci.subnational: Province: Chiang Mai (1 of 77 provinces); single urban-community household survey
mci.representative: community (regional); urban Chiang Mai households aged 30-65 — NOT national, NOT elderly-restricted

scd.criteria: structured-questionnaire (self-reported memory-complaint framework); NOT single-item — hence non-inflated 7.05% rather than the 40-60% typical of single self-report questions
scd.citation: Pinyopornpanish K, et al. (2024). A household survey of the prevalence of subjective cognitive decline and mild cognitive impairment among urban community-dwelling adults aged 30 to 65. Scientific Reports (Nature). SCD = 7.05% of n=539, urban Chiang Mai. Structured memory-complaint questionnaire (not single-item). Only available Thailand SCD estimate.
scd.subnational: Province: Chiang Mai (1 of 77 provinces); single urban-community household survey
scd.representative: community (regional); urban Chiang Mai households aged 30-65 — only usable Thai SCD figure, not national/elderly

mci.criteria: Neuropsychological battery, >1.5 SD below norms on >=1 of 5 cognitive domains (Petersen/DSM-5 style; single-domain 27.8%, multi-domain 24.3%). CLINIC SAMPLE OVERESTIMATES - not a community rate.
mci.citation: Prevalence and risk factors of mild cognitive impairment in a tertiary care setting in Turkey. Z Gerontol Geriatr 2023. Single tertiary geriatric outpatient clinic, n=259 community-dwelling older adults, mean age 74; overall MCI 52.1%. NOTE community seed VERIFIED: Senturk I et al., Prevalence of Dementia and MCI in a Rural Area of Sivas, Turkey, Cureus 2021;13(2):e13069 (PMC7930887) - door-to-door, n=500, aged 60+, MMSE-based; reports dementia 16.8% but MCI% not in indexed abstract (need full text; MMSE underestimates MCI). Triangulation: Kayseri community study n=900 cognitive impairment 26.1%.
mci.representative: clinic (single tertiary geriatric outpatient clinic; n=259; NOT nationally representative; inflates vs community)

scd.criteria: self-report-single-item (subjective memory complaints, SMC) - INFLATES; not the structured SCD-I framework
scd.citation: Cetin M, Sarikaya Demirbas Z, Naharci MI. Subjective memory complaints among older adults in Turkiye: prevalence and associated factors. Turk J Med Sci 2025 (pub 2025-09-22). Retrospective cross-sectional; n=500 community-dwelling older adults assessed at a tertiary geriatric outpatient clinic (Ankara). SMC prevalence 61.0%; median age 76; 64% female. First study of SMC prevalence in Turkey. PMC12779049.
scd.representative: clinic (single tertiary geriatric outpatient clinic, Ankara; n=500; not nationally representative)

mci.criteria: Clinical diagnosis via door-to-door interview using NIA-AA clinical criteria + CDR + AD8 screen; NOT MoCA-only (so not the ~20%+ MoCA overestimate artifact). 18.76% is age-adjusted.
mci.citation: Sun Y, Lee HJ, Yang SC, et al. A Nationwide Survey of Mild Cognitive Impairment and Dementia, Including Very Mild Dementia, in Taiwan. PLoS One 2014;9(6):e100303. PMID 24940604. n=10,432 aged 65+ (mean 76.2, 52.3% women), computerized random sampling from all 19 counties. Age-adjusted MCI 18.76% (95% CI 17.91–19.61); women 21.63% vs men 15.57%. Seed VERIFIED and retained as best national source.
mci.subnational: Urban vs rural strata reported; 19 counties sampled (rural-urban disparity analyses published from same nationwide door-to-door dataset)
mci.representative: national — computerized random sampling across all 19 Taiwan counties, community-dwelling elderly

scd.criteria: self-report-single-item (single question on forgetfulness re: taking medication regularly), item choice informed by the SCD-I Working Group framework. Not a full SCD-I structured battery; single-item, but value (24.3%) is NOT in the inflated 40–60% range typical of naive single-item SMC.
scd.citation: Independent association between subjective cognitive decline and frailty in the elderly. PLoS One 2018;13(8):e0201351. PMID 30071051. Healthy Aging Longitudinal Study in Taiwan (HALST), n=2,386 aged 65+ (mean 73.23, 51.3% women); 579 SCD / 1,807 normal cognition → SCD prevalence 24.3%. IMPROVEMENT over prior seed Wang/Fuh 2000 Kinmen SMC (PMID 10733056, longitudinal SMC study, no clean national prevalence, rural single-county). HALST is larger, more recent, national ageing cohort.
scd.representative: national cohort (HALST) — community-dwelling older adults recruited across multiple sites in Taiwan; cohort-based, not a formal prevalence survey

mci.criteria: Two-phase design: screen + full assessment with DSM-IV / Petersen-type clinical consensus MCI diagnosis (IDEA study). Not MoCA-only; clinically adjudicated, so not over-inflated.
mci.citation: Paddick SM, Kisoli A, Samuel M, et al. Mild Cognitive Impairment in Rural Tanzania: Prevalence, Profile, and Outcomes at 4-Year Follow-up. Am J Geriatr Psychiatry 2015;23(9):950-959. Rural Hai district. Over 4y, 32.6% progressed to dementia.
mci.representative: community (single rural district, Hai — not national)
mci.n: 1198 screened phase I; 296 fully assessed phase II

mci.criteria: IDEA cognitive screening tool + IDEA-IADL functional assessment; MCI = impaired cognition with preserved function. Screening/algorithm-based, not full clinical consensus. (12% dementia; 20.6% any neurocognitive impairment overall.)
mci.citation: Prevalence and correlates of neurocognitive impairment among older persons in rural Eastern Uganda. npj Dementia 2026;s44400-026-00077-9. n=598, 60.6% female, 39% no formal education.
mci.representative: community (rural Eastern Uganda districts — not national)

mci.criteria: HCAP neuropsychological battery with algorithmic (Langa-Weir/HCAP) classification — comprehensive battery, NOT MoCA-only; MCI defined by cognitive test performance below norms without dementia
mci.citation: Manly JJ, Jones RN, Langa KM, et al. Estimating the Prevalence of Dementia and Mild Cognitive Impairment in the US: The 2016 Health and Retirement Study Harmonized Cognitive Assessment Protocol (HCAP) Project. JAMA Neurology. 2022;79(12):1242-1249. Nationally representative: MCI 22%, dementia 10% among adults 65+. Triangulation: Mayo Clinic Study of Aging (Petersen et al., Neurology 2010;75:889-897) reported MCI prevalence 16.0% (95% CI 14.4-17.5), ages 70-89, n=1969, regional (Olmsted County MN) — seed verified but superseded by nationally representative HCAP.
mci.representative: national — nationally representative sample of community-dwelling US adults 65+ (HRS-HCAP)

scd.criteria: self-report-single-item — BRFSS Cognitive Decline module single question ('During the past 12 months, have you experienced confusion or memory loss that is happening more often or is getting worse?'); this is a conservatively worded single item, so 11.2% is far below the 40-60% inflation range typical of open SCD questions, but it remains single-item self-report (not SCD-I structured framework)
scd.citation: Taylor CA, Bouldin ED, McGuire LC. Subjective Cognitive Decline Among Adults Aged ≥45 Years — United States, 2015–2016. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep. 2018;67(27):753-757. Overall 11.2% of adults ≥45 reported SCD; 50.6% of those reported SCD-related functional limitations. Seed verified (PMC6047468). More recent CDC/BRFSS: age-adjusted SCD 9.6% for 2015-2020 (MMWR 2023, mm7210a1).
scd.subnational: state — BRFSS is state-based; state-level SCD prevalence available across 50 states + DC (this MMWR reports the national aggregate)
scd.representative: national — BRFSS non-institutionalized US adults ≥45 (optional Cognitive Decline module, large multi-state sample)
scd.n: large national BRFSS sample (2015-2016 optional Cognitive Decline module; exact n not stated in abstract)

mci.criteria: 10/66 aMCI (Mayo Clinic amnestic MCI algorithm) — strict amnestic-only, UNDERESTIMATES all-type MCI. Pooled crude aMCI 0.8–4.3% (standardized 0.6–4.6%); exact Venezuela value in full-text Table 1 — need full text. (Maracaibo Aging Study, Maestre et al., reports dementia 8.04% but NO MCI figure.)
mci.citation: Sosa AL et al. 10/66 MCI study, PLOS Med. 2012;9(2):e1001170 (Venezuela catchment). Complementary: Maracaibo Aging Study (Maestre GE et al.) — dementia 8.04%, MCI not reported.
mci.representative: regional (10/66 Venezuela catchment; Maracaibo Aging Study = downtown Maracaibo, Zulia)

mci.criteria: MMSE + Lawton IADL (screen-detected MCI). TRAP: clinic/outpatient sample selection inflates prevalence; MMSE alone tends to underestimate MCI in community but hospital selection more than offsets this. Not Petersen/DSM-5 formal MCI.
mci.citation: Nguyen KQ, Than THN, To KG (2026). Prevalence and Correlates of Mild Cognitive Impairment in Older Outpatients: A Cross-Sectional Study. INQUIRY (SAGE). Screen-detected MCI in 30.1% of 631 adults aged >=60 attending the outpatient dept of Nhan Dan Gia Dinh Hospital, Ho Chi Minh City (southern Vietnam), May 2024-Feb 2025; MMSE + Lawton IADL. CLINIC-based, overestimates community prevalence. Community triangulation: Nguyen TN et al., IJERPH 2021 (PMC7915207), Thua Thien Hue province, n=725 community-dwelling >=60, MMSE -> mild cognitive impairment 23.6% (severe 19.3%). Suggests community MCI ~24% vs clinic 30.1%.
mci.subnational: province-level data exists: Thua Thien Hue community study (1 province, 23.6%); this primary record is single-hospital in Ho Chi Minh City
mci.representative: clinic (single hospital outpatient department, HCMC, southern Vietnam; not nationally representative)

scd.criteria: self-report-single-item (subjective memory complaints, NOT SCD-I structured framework). TRAP: single self-report items inflate. 64% rated memory 'at least poor'; 39% reported memory interfered with daily life at least somewhat (interference-based figure closer to concern-qualified SCD).
scd.year: 2018 (IntPsychogeriatr vol 30 issue 7; data collected ~2016-17). NOTE: ScienceDirect PII date 2024 is an Elsevier re-host, NOT the study year.
scd.citation: Prevalence and correlates of subjective memory complaints in Vietnamese adults. International Psychogeriatrics 2018;30(7). Stratified community sample n=600 adults aged >=55 in Da Nang city + surrounding areas (central Vietnam), home interviews. 64% reported at least poor memory; 39% said memory interfered with daily life. SMC single self-report items (not SCD-I) -> inflated -> low confidence. The seed URL sciencedirect.com/.../S1041610224017587 is the Elsevier re-host of this 2018 article; OA author manuscript = PMC5986585.
scd.access: OA (author manuscript, PMC5986585); journal version paywalled(Cambridge/Elsevier)
scd.subnational: regional only: Da Nang city + surrounding areas (central Vietnam), single-region; no national/multi-unit SCD data
scd.representative: community (stratified community sample, Da Nang region; not national)

mci.criteria: MMSE <=23 (neurocognitive dysfunction in a Type-2-diabetes clinic sample; NOT general-population MCI)
mci.citation: Prevalence of Neurocognitive Dysfunction and Associated Risk Factors Among Follow-up Type 2 Diabetic Patients, Al-Thawra Hospital Diabetes Center, Sana'a, Yemen, Front Endocrinol 2026; MMSE<=23, ~43% among T2DM patients — abstract only, need full text for age/n breakdown
mci.representative: clinic (diabetes-center T2DM patients; not general population)

mci.criteria: NIA-AA core clinical criteria operationalized via SAGE cognitive battery (verbal immediate/delayed recall, digit span, verbal fluency); NOT MoCA-only, so no MoCA overestimation trap
mci.citation: Smith L, Il Shin J, Jackson SE, et al. Food Insecurity Is Associated with Mild Cognitive Impairment among Middle-Aged and Older Adults in South Africa: Findings from a Nationally Representative Survey (WHO SAGE Wave 1). Nutrients. 2019;11(4):749. MCI prevalence 8.5% among n=3,672 adults aged >=50.
mci.representative: national (WHO SAGE South Africa Wave 1, nationally representative sample of adults aged >=50)

mci.criteria: 10/66 dementia screening protocol, 'intermediate' band (dfscore 0.12 to <0.184) used as MCI proxy — NOT validated Petersen MCI; CHW-administered pilot. NB same instrument flagged 54% as possible dementia (clear screening overestimate), so MCI figure is soft.
mci.citation: Mataa M, et al. Using community health workers to assess the prevalence of dementia in a community based sample of older Zambians. Alzheimer's & Dementia 2024 (conf. abstract / PMC). 2 high-density Lusaka communities; n=185. need full text for validated MCI.
mci.representative: community (2 Lusaka high-density areas; pilot, non-representative)
